metadata.summary: Observe real users attempting predefined tasks on a product, measure task completion and time, identify usability problems, and prioritize fixes by severity so you launch without critical blockers.

content.title: Usability Testing
content.summary: A structured method for observing real users attempt predefined tasks with a product. Sessions measure effectiveness (task completion), efficiency (time on task), and satisfaction (post-task ratings). Findings are prioritized by severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low) and reported with direct recommendations. Minimum 5 participants per segment for pattern detection.

why.p: Products built on assumptions consistently have usability problems that only emerge under real use. Usability testing catches critical issues before launch, when fixes are cheap, and produces evidence-based severity ratings that force prioritization. Five users typically reveal 85% of major problems (Nielsen, 1993) — making it high-value even on a limited budget.

list.item: Before a major feature launch — validate that task flows work for real users.
When conversion or activation metrics are low with no clear analytics explanation.
When redesigning navigation or an information architecture — test with think-aloud protocol.
After implementing a competitor's pattern — verify it works for your users, not just theirs.
When stakeholders disagree on a design direction — let test results arbitrate.
When you need statistical significance across hundreds of users — use unmoderated studies with larger samples or quantitative A/B testing instead.
When the question is "what should we build?" — use generative research (interviews, diary studies) before usability testing.
On a prototype fidelity too low to support realistic tasks — results reflect prototype gaps, not product usability.

section.name: step-1-define-goals
section.p: The first step is to define what you want to know. Goals must be answerable questions tied to metrics, not vague topics.

rule.statement: State test goals as answerable questions, not topics. Each question must be tied to a measurable metric.
rule.rationale: Vague goals ("understand navigation") produce unactionable findings. Specific questions ("Can users complete checkout in under 3 minutes?") produce pass/fail evidence.

section.p: Examples of good research questions: Can users complete checkout in under 3 minutes? Do users understand the navigation structure on first use? What blocks users from signing up? Do users find the settings menu without help?

section.name: step-2-create-test-plan
section.p: Before recruiting a single participant, document the full test plan: objectives, methodology, participant criteria, tasks, metrics, and schedule. Recruiting before planning means participants may not match the tasks.

rule.statement: Document objectives, participant profile, tasks, metrics, and environment before recruiting. Include session duration, success criteria per task, and the analysis method (moderated vs. unmoderated).
rule.rationale: Recruiting before task design means participants may not match the tasks. Plan first, recruit second. A written plan prevents scope creep and ensures consistency across sessions.

section.name: step-3-write-tasks
section.p: Tasks must be realistic scenarios with clear goals and zero hints about the solution path. A task that hints at the UI (e.g., "click the Account button") measures the participant's ability to follow instructions, not their ability to navigate the interface.

rule.statement: Tasks must use realistic scenarios with clear goals and zero hints about the solution path. Task format: Scenario: [context and motivation]. Task: [what to accomplish]. Success: [measurable completion].
rule.rationale: Hints in task wording ("click the Account button") inflate task success rates and produce false confidence in design quality. Scenario framing activates realistic decision-making; decontextualized tasks feel artificial.

rule.statement: Never include UI element names, menu labels, or step sequences in a task instruction. Do not say "Click the menu and select Orders"; instead say "Find your recent order and update the shipping address".
rule.rationale: Including solution steps measures comprehension of instructions, not usability of the interface.

example.code: Scenario: You need to change your delivery address for an upcoming order. Task: Find your recent order and update the shipping address. Success: User locates order and changes address without prompting.
example.note: No UI element names. User must discover the path themselves. Clear success criteria.

example.code: Task: "Click the account button and select orders." (Gives away the solution path; measures ability to follow instructions, not ability to navigate.)
example.note: Explicit instructions inflate success rates and hide real usability problems.

section.name: step-4-recruit-participants
section.p: Participant quality determines finding quality. Users must match your target user profile, and you need enough participants per segment to detect recurring patterns.

rule.statement: Recruit 5-8 participants per distinct user segment; match the target user profile including experience level mix. Segments with different mental models must be tested separately (e.g., iOS users and Android users if you support both platforms).
rule.rationale: Fewer than 5 participants per segment misses recurring patterns. Fewer than 5 also means one outlier skews results. Five participants reveal approximately 85% of major usability problems (Nielsen research).

section.name: step-5-conduct-sessions
section.p: During the session, your job is to observe, not to help. Any facilitator intervention (answering questions, guiding navigation, or reacting to errors) invalidates the task result.

rule.statement: Facilitate without helping: do not answer questions, guide navigation, or react to errors during tasks. If a user is stuck, say "There is no right answer; try what feels natural" and wait. If they truly cannot proceed, mark the task as failed and move on.
rule.rationale: Any facilitator intervention invalidates the task result — it measures the facilitator's skill, not the product's usability. Patience and silence are core skills for a usability moderator.

section.p: Session structure: Introduction: 5 minutes (establish rapport, explain think-aloud if using it) Pre-test questions: 5 minutes (gather demographic context) Tasks: 30-45 minutes (participant attempts each task while you observe) Post-task questions: 10 minutes (satisfaction ratings, follow-up probes) Debrief: 5 minutes (overall impressions, willingness to recommend)

section.name: step-6-analyze-results
section.p: After all sessions, analyze findings and assign severity ratings. Unrated findings produce undifferentiated lists that teams cannot prioritize.

rule.statement: Assign severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low) to every finding before reporting. Severity definition: Critical = users cannot complete the task; High = significant difficulty completing; Medium = some struggle; Low = minor friction.
rule.rationale: Unrated findings produce undifferentiated lists that teams cannot prioritize. Severity ratings force triage and align effort with impact.

list.item: Critical — users cannot complete the task. Fix immediately.
High — significant difficulty completing. Fix before launch.
Medium — some struggle. Fix soon after launch.
Low — minor friction. Fix when possible.

section.name: step-7-report-and-act
section.p: Findings without recommendations leave design decisions to whoever reads the report. Researchers must propose the fix.

rule.statement: Each finding must include: severity, affected task, frequency (N of Y participants), problem description, direct quote or evidence, and a concrete recommendation.
rule.rationale: Findings without recommendations leave design decisions to whoever reads the report. Researchers must propose the fix to ensure findings drive action.

section.name: moderated-vs-unmoderated
section.p: The format of testing (moderated or unmoderated) depends on your research questions and budget.

rule.statement: Use moderated sessions for complex, exploratory research where follow-up probing is needed; use unmoderated for validation at scale. Moderated: facilitator present, can probe, fewer sessions, higher cost, deeper insight. Unmoderated: remote automated, pre-set questions only, scales to many sessions, lower cost, broader patterns.
rule.rationale: Moderated sessions surface the why behind behavior; unmoderated provides statistical breadth. Choosing the wrong format wastes budget.

section.name: common-facilitator-mistakes
section.p: Moderators are the instrument of usability testing. Poor moderation invalidates results.

antipattern.description: Helping a user who is stuck by pointing at the correct area or saying "try the menu".
antipattern.reason: Any help makes the finding invalid. Record the failure; do not rescue it. The user's struggle is the finding.

antipattern.description: Recruiting participants who do not match the target user profile (e.g., testing a parenting app with childless users).
antipattern.reason: Results reflect the recruited population, not the actual user base. Wrong participants produce misleading findings.

antipattern.description: Collecting only qualitative observations without any quantitative metrics (no task success rates, no time-on-task).
antipattern.reason: Purely subjective findings are difficult to prioritize and impossible to track over time. Always measure: task success (binary: completed without help), time on task, and satisfaction (numeric scale).

antipattern.description: Fixing issues mid-study ("We noticed this bug in session 1, let's fix it before session 2").
antipattern.reason: Fixing mid-study invalidates comparative data across sessions. Pause recruiting, fix the bug, then resume with clear documentation of the intervention point. Or accept the inconsistency and note it in the report.
